perturbation.original_text: Information disclosed orally or visually and identified at the time as proprietary or confidential shall be covered hereunder if it is reduced to writing, marked as provided herein, and transmitted to Receiving Party within thirty (30) days after disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Information disclosed orally or visually and identified at the time as proprietary or confidential shall be covered hereunder if it is reduced to writing, marked as provided herein, and transmitted to Receiving Party within ninety (90) days after disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'thirty (30) days' to 'ninety (90) days' alters the timeframe for protecting orally disclosed confidential information. This can create ambiguity and potential unenforceability, especially if trade secrets are involved and a shorter timeframe is customary or legally required.
perturbation.law_explanation: While CUTSA doesn't explicitly define a timeframe for documenting oral disclosures, a 90-day delay could be deemed unreasonable in swiftly evolving industries, potentially weakening trade secret protection claims. The modified text might not provide 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, a key element for trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for documenting oral disclosures from 30 to 90 days. While the provided law snippet (California Code, CIV 3426) doesn't explicitly define a timeframe, the Law Explanation suggests that a 90-day delay could be deemed unreasonable, especially in fast-paced industries. This potentially weakens trade secret protection claims because it might not demonstrate 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. The Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The 90 day timeframe could be viewed as misaligned with the standard of 'reasonable efforts', creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Because of the trade secret subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for ninety (90) days thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Because of the subject matter of Entera's business, Disclosee agrees that it will not solicit the services of any of the employees of Entera during the term of this Agreement and for one (1) year thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the non-solicitation period from 'ninety (90) days' to 'one (1) year' creates a potential restraint of trade issue, especially in California, where such agreements are narrowly construed. This could render the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, and extended non-solicitation agreements can be viewed as de facto non-competes if they unduly restrict an employee's ability to work. A one-year restriction is more likely to be challenged and deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade under California law than the original 90 days.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to one year, which is a significant increase from the original 90 days. According to the provided law explanation, California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, and extended non-solicitation agreements can be viewed as de facto non-competes. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BPC 16600(a)) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." Extending the non-solicitation period to one year could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, conflicting with California law as it may unduly restrict an employee's ability to work. Therefore, the modification introduces a legal contradiction by potentially violating California's prohibition of non-compete agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party, upon two (2) days notice in writing to the other Party, may terminate the Agreement with respect to disclosures made thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party, upon verbal notice to the other Party, may terminate the Agreement with respect to disclosures made thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'two (2) days notice in writing' to 'verbal notice' introduces ambiguity and contradicts standard contract interpretation practices. Verbal notice is harder to prove and less reliable, creating uncertainty in the termination process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code Section 1624 - Statute of Frauds
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly applicable as this isn't necessarily an agreement that *must* be in writing under the Statute of Frauds, the change undermines the principles of clear contractual terms and written evidence, which are important in contract law. Courts generally prefer written notice for termination to avoid disputes over whether notice was properly given.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.justification: The change from 'two (2) days notice in writing' to 'verbal notice' directly contradicts principles of contract law that favor written evidence to avoid disputes. Although CIV 1624 primarily addresses contracts required to be in writing under the Statute of Frauds, the underlying principle of written documentation for clear contractual terms extends to termination clauses as well. The original text provided a verifiable record of notice, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and potential disputes regarding whether notice was actually given. The altered language undermines the enforceability and reliability of the termination provision. Therefore, it is a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: As used herein the term "Information" includes any and all technology, information, knowledge or data whether oral, written, in a physical form or otherwise, which is disclosed for the purpose set forth above and which is identified by the Disclosing Party at the time of disclosure as being proprietary and/or confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein the term "Information" includes only written documents clearly marked as proprietary and/or confidential, exchanged at formal meetings. No other form of communication shall be included.
perturbation.explanation: Narrowing the definition of 'Information' to 'only written documents' severely restricts the scope of protection. It excludes oral disclosures, physical forms, and other data, which are commonly protected under NDA's. It also requires the exchange to happen in formal meetings, which is unusual.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modified definition undermines the protection of trade secrets under CUTSA. A crucial aspect of trade secret law is the protection of *information* that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. Limiting 'Information' to only written, formally exchanged documents drastically reduces the scope of what can be protected as a trade secret, making it far easier for a receiving party to argue that certain disclosures are not covered by the NDA, and thus not protected under CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Information' compared to the original text, limiting it to only written documents exchanged at formal meetings. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the protection of trade secrets under CUTSA because it drastically reduces what can be protected. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), indicating that California law protects trade secrets. The modified text contradicts the broad protection afforded by trade secret law by limiting the definition of information, thus making it easier to argue that certain disclosures are not protected.
